1: ʾašrê kol-yĕrēʾ yhwh hahōlēk bidrākāyw — "blessed is every person who fears the LORD and walks in his ways" — Ps 128 is a companion beatitude to Ps 112 (both begin with ʾašrê and focus on the God-fearing man's flourishing). The universal kol (every, all) opens the blessing to any person, not just the head of household — though the psalm will develop the domestic picture. hahōlēk bidrākāyw (the one who walks in his ways) — the walking (hālak) metaphor for covenant life is Deuteronomic in origin (Deuteronomy 8:6; Psalms 10:12).
2: yĕgîaʿ kappêkā kî tōʾkēl ʾašrêkā wĕṭôb lāk — "you will eat the fruit of your labor; you will be blessed, and things will go well for you" — yĕgîaʿ kappêkā (the toil/labor of your palms/hands) — kap (palm, hand, hollow of the hand) — the very hands that work. Contrasting with Psalms 127:2 (which warns against anxious toil), Ps 128 says that the labor of the God-fearer is not vain labor but genuinely productive. The covenant blessing is concrete and physical: the hands that work will eat what they have produced.
3: ʾeštĕkā kĕgepen pōriyyāh bĕyarkĕtê bêtĕkā bānêkā kĕšĕtîlê zêtîm sābîb lĕšulḥānĕkā — "your wife will be like a fruitful vine within your home; your children like young olive shoots around your table" — gepen pōriyyāh (a fruitful vine) — the vine bearing fruit in abundance; fertility imagery from the agricultural blessings of the land. bĕyarkĕtê bêtĕkā (within the innermost parts/sides of your house) — not a public display but flourishing in the domestic interior. šĕtîlê zêtîm (shoots/seedlings of olive trees) — young olives require years to mature but then produce abundantly for generations; the children are investments in the future, not merely present joys.
4: hinnēh kî-kēn yĕbōrak gāber yĕrēʾ yhwh — "behold, thus shall the man be blessed who fears the LORD" — hinnēh kî-kēn (behold, for so, thus indeed) — the emphatic pointing-back formula: what was described in vv2-3 is precisely the content of the blessing. The verse functions as a summary and validation of the domestic vision: this is not an aspirational fantasy but the structural pattern of covenant life when lived from the fear of the LORD.
5: yĕbārekkā yhwh miṣṣiyyôn ûrĕʾēh bĕṭûb yĕrûšālāim kōl yĕmê ḥayyêkā — "may the LORD bless you from Zion; may you see Jerusalem flourish all the days of your life" — the blessing source is miṣṣiyyôn (from Zion) — YHWH blesses from his chosen dwelling place. The connection between the domestic (wife and children, vv2-3) and the civic (Jerusalem) is deliberate: the individual household's flourishing is part of the city's flourishing and vice versa. ûrĕʾēh bĕṭûb yĕrûšālāim (and see the good of Jerusalem) — to live long enough to see the city well is itself a blessing.
6: ûrĕʾēh-bānîm lĕbānêkā šālôm ʿal-yiśrāʾēl — "may you live to see your grandchildren. Peace be upon Israel" — bānîm lĕbānêkā (sons of your sons, i.e., grandchildren) — longevity enough to see the third generation. In the ancient world, dying before your grandchildren were born was a sign of divine disfavor; seeing them was the fullness of covenant blessing (Genesis 50:23; Job 42:16). The closing šālôm ʿal-yiśrāʾēl (peace be upon Israel) — the standard liturgical coda of the Songs of Ascents (Psalms 125:5; Psalms 131:3), extending the individual household's blessing to the whole people.
